title: Movement as Lifetime Practice, Coaching & Programming
pillar_name: Physical Literacy, Martial Arts & Human Performance

suggested.title: [Book]
suggested.author: Dr. Kelly Starrett and Juliet Starrett

competence_markers.description: The student is actively progressing in at least 3 advanced bodyweight skills (muscle-up, handstand push-up, front lever, pistol squat, L-sit)
The student can describe at least 3 movement systems, explain what each prioritizes, and articulate what their own practice draws from each
The student can coach a beginner through a squat, push-up, and dead hang with appropriate cues and corrections
The student has designed and executed a 4-week movement program with deload protocol
The student can explain the concept of training for longevity — why training at 16 should serve the body at 60
The student can identify common injury patterns (ACL, rotator cuff, lumbar, ankle) and explain the prehab approach for each
The student maintains an independent movement and mobility practice without external direction
The student demonstrates movement quality that is visibly superior to an untrained peer — they move with control, efficiency, and awareness

cross_connections.description: Strength programming and movement programming integrate at this tier. The student designs a complete physical practice, not separate strength and movement programs.
Coaching younger students is leadership practice. The ability to teach physical skills requires patience, observation, communication, and the capacity to meet someone where they are.
Maintaining an independent training practice through periods of low motivation is the purest expression of IC2 discipline.
Understanding biomechanics, injury mechanisms, and training adaptation is applied science. The critical thinking from 1.6 applies directly to evaluating movement claims and training methods.

content.why_this_matters:
You have spent years building movement competence. You can move well. You understand your body's mechanics. You have the bodyweight skills, the mobility habits, and the physical literacy that most adults never develop.

Now comes the question that matters for the rest of your life: how do you keep this? How do you design a movement practice that serves you at 20, 40, 60, and beyond? How do you train at 16 in a way that builds the body you want at 50 instead of destroying it? And how do you teach what you know to someone who does not yet have it?

This packet is about the long game. It is about understanding training as a decades-long practice rather than a season-long effort. It is about learning multiple movement systems — gymnastics, MovNat, yoga, parkour, Ido Portal's movement culture — and understanding what each offers and what each neglects. It is about developing the coaching eye that can watch someone move, identify what is wrong, and cue the correction. And it is about designing your own movement programming with intelligence, not just enthusiasm.

The person who completes this packet does not need a coach to tell them what to do. They understand the principles well enough to coach themselves — and to coach others.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Advanced bodyweight skills** — muscle-ups, handstand push-ups, front levers, pistol squats, L-sits as demonstrations of total body control
2. **Movement system literacy** — understanding multiple movement philosophies and what each prioritizes
3. **The coaching eye** — the ability to watch someone move, identify dysfunction, and cue correction
4. **Programming for longevity** — designing training that serves the body across decades, not just weeks
5. **Injury prevention and prehab** — understanding common injury patterns and the training approaches that reduce their likelihood
6. **Teaching competence** — the ability to coach a younger student through basic movement patterns
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Advanced Bodyweight Skills

These skills are not party tricks. They are demonstrations of total body control — the integration of strength, mobility, balance, and coordination that distinguishes someone who has mastered their body from someone who is merely fit.

**Muscle-up:** A pull-up that transitions to a dip at the top — pulling yourself from below the bar to above it in one movement. Requires explosive pulling power and a deep dip at the transition point. Progression: strict pull-ups → high pull-ups (chest to bar) → muscle-up negatives (jump above the bar, lower through the transition slowly) → kipping muscle-up → strict muscle-up.

**Handstand push-up:** A push-up performed upside down, in a handstand against a wall. Requires shoulder strength, thoracic mobility, and body control. Progression: pike push-ups → elevated pike push-ups → wall handstand hold → wall handstand negatives (lower slowly) → wall handstand push-ups → freestanding handstand push-ups.

**Front lever:** Hanging from a bar with your body horizontal, face up. Requires extraordinary core and lat strength. Progression: dead hang → tuck front lever → advanced tuck → straddle front lever → full front lever. This takes months to years.

**Pistol squat:** A full-depth single-leg squat. Requires ankle mobility, hip flexibility, quad and glute strength, and balance. If you have been progressing through Weathering Storms, you are near this. Refine it here.

**L-sit:** Seated on the floor or parallettes, lift your body off the ground with straight arms and legs extended in front of you. Requires core compression strength and tricep endurance. Progress from tuck L-sit to full L-sit, building hold time.

**The standard:** You do not need to master all of these. You need to be actively progressing in all of them and competent in most. These skills are long-term projects — some will take years. That is the point. Training is a practice, not a checklist.

### Part 2: Movement System Literacy

There is no single movement system that develops everything the human body can do. Each system prioritizes certain qualities and neglects others. A complete mover understands multiple systems and draws from each.

**Gymnastics:** Prioritizes strength, flexibility, body line, and precision. Develops extraordinary upper-body control and body awareness. Neglects: natural terrain movement, locomotion, real-world carrying and climbing. The gymnast on the rings is remarkable; the gymnast on a mountain trail may be unremarkable.

**MovNat / Natural Movement:** Prioritizes practical, real-world movement — running, climbing, jumping, crawling, swimming, carrying, throwing in natural environments. Develops functional capability across all human movement patterns. Neglects: the high-end strength and precision that gymnastics and structured training produce.

**Yoga:** Prioritizes flexibility, breath control, body awareness, and the mind-body connection. Develops extraordinary range of motion and internal awareness. Neglects: strength, power, and the ability to apply physical capability under external resistance.

**Parkour:** Prioritizes environmental reading, efficient locomotion, and the ability to overcome physical obstacles. Develops agility, courage, and problem-solving with the body. Neglects: sustained strength, mobility maintenance, and slow controlled movement.

**Ido Portal Method:** Attempts to integrate all movement qualities — strength, mobility, locomotion, improvisation, partner work, dance. Philosophically ambitious. Demands significant training volume. Neglects: nothing deliberately, but the breadth means depth in any single area may lag behind a specialist.

**Your task:** Experience each of these systems — through classes, workshops, videos, or self-study. Understand what each offers. Then design your own practice that draws from multiple systems based on your goals, your body, and what you enjoy. A person who trains only one system is like a person who reads only one genre — competent within their domain, limited outside it.

### Part 3: The Coaching Eye

Watching someone move and identifying what is wrong is a higher-order skill that requires deep understanding of what right looks like. This is how you develop it:

**Step 1: Know the standard.** For every fundamental movement — squat, hinge, push, pull, overhead reach, rotation — you need a clear picture of what correct looks like. Joint angles, body lines, tempo, stability. Study *Becoming a Supple Leopard* and *Movement* for these standards.

**Step 2: Watch from multiple angles.** Front, side, and back views reveal different things. A squat that looks good from the front may show an asymmetric weight shift from behind. A push-up that looks good from the side may show winging scapulae from behind.

**Step 3: Identify the primary fault.** Most movement errors have a root cause that produces secondary effects. Knees collapsing inward during a squat (the visible fault) may be caused by weak glutes, tight ankles, or poor hip mobility (the root cause). Fix the root cause, not the symptom.

**Step 4: Cue simply.** A good coaching cue is one sentence: "drive your knees out," "chest up," "squeeze your glutes at the top," "land quiet." The student cannot process a paragraph of instructions while moving. One cue at a time. The most important cue first.

**Practice:** Coach a younger student or a family member through a squat, a push-up, and a dead hang. Watch them. Identify what needs correction. Give one cue. See if it works. This is a skill that develops through practice, not through reading about it.

### Part 4: Programming for Longevity

Training at 16 should serve the body at 60. This is the principle that separates intelligent training from foolish training.

**Training age matters more than chronological age.** A 16-year-old with 8 years of training has a different body than a 16-year-old with 1 year of training. Programming should reflect training age, not just physical age.

**Joint health is the long game.** A muscle heals in weeks. A tendon heals in months. Cartilage may not heal at all. Training that loads joints beyond their current capacity — through excessive volume, insufficient mobility, or poor technique — pays its cost years later. The student who trains hard but maintains mobility, respects deload weeks, and never sacrifices form for weight is investing in decades of capability. The student who trains recklessly may perform well at 20 and be broken at 35.

**Deload weeks are not weakness.** Every 4–6 weeks, reduce training volume and intensity by 40–60%. Your body adapts during recovery, not during training. A deload week is when the adaptations from the previous training block are consolidated. Skipping deloads leads to overreaching, which leads to stagnation or injury.

**Variety prevents overuse.** No single movement pattern should be loaded heavily every session, indefinitely. Rotate emphasis. Cycle between strength-focused, skill-focused, and mobility-focused phases. The body adapts to variety and breaks down under monotony.

### Part 5: Injury Prevention and Prehab

**Common injury patterns and their prevention:**

- **ACL tears** — most common in cutting sports; prevented by strengthening the hamstrings and glutes, developing proper landing and cutting mechanics, and addressing knee valgus (inward collapse) under fatigue
- **Rotator cuff injuries** — caused by overhead work with insufficient shoulder mobility or scapular stability; prevented by daily shoulder mobility work, band pull-aparts, and external rotation strengthening
- **Lumbar disc injuries** — caused by flexing the lower back under load (rounding during deadlifts, squats, or carries); prevented by maintaining hip mobility, bracing the core under load, and respecting the lumbar spine's need for stability
- **Ankle sprains** — caused by poor proprioception and insufficient ankle strength; prevented by single-leg balance work, ankle mobility drills, and training on uneven surfaces

**Prehab principle:** It is easier and less painful to prevent an injury than to rehabilitate one. 10 minutes of targeted prehab daily costs you almost nothing. A torn ACL costs you 6–12 months and may never fully resolve. The math is not complicated.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Advanced Bodyweight Skills
**Developing well:** Active progression in 3+ skills. Form is prioritized over completion. The student understands that front levers and muscle-ups are long-term projects and trains them patiently.

**Developing slowly:** Attempts advanced skills without proper progressions. Sacrifices form for Instagram-worthy attempts. Frustrated by slow progress.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student following a progression, or attempting the end skill repeatedly? Progressions work. Repeated failure at the end skill without building intermediate strength does not.

### 2. Movement System Literacy
**Developing well:** Can articulate the priorities and limitations of 3+ systems from personal experience. Has integrated elements from multiple systems into their own practice. Avoids dogma — does not claim one system is "the best."

**Developing slowly:** Has theoretical knowledge but no practical experience with multiple systems. Or has become dogmatic about one approach.

### 3. Coaching Ability
**Developing well:** Coaches with simple, effective cues. Identifies the primary fault before secondary faults. Adjusts approach when a cue does not work. The student being coached visibly improves.

**Developing slowly:** Over-explains. Gives multiple cues simultaneously. Misses obvious faults. Cannot simplify complex concepts for a younger learner.

### 4. Self-Directed Programming
**Developing well:** Has designed and executed a multi-week program with logical structure, progressive overload, deload, and variety. Can explain the reasoning behind every programming decision.

**Developing slowly:** Program is random — a collection of exercises without structure. Cannot explain why they chose the volume, intensity, or exercise selection they did. Skips deloads.
